standard.title: Food Webs and Ecosystems
standard.description: Use models, including food webs, to represent matter and energy flow in ecosystems and predict the impact of changing abiotic and biotic factors on populations
standard.prior_knowledge: Students can identify producers, consumers and decomposers and construct simple food chains. They have not studied energy flow quantitatively or modelled multi-factor ecosystem changes.
standard.progression_endpoint: Students use food web models to represent matter and energy flow, and predict and explain population impacts of changes to abiotic and biotic factors.

nodes.label: From food chains to food webs
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can construct a food web and identify the roles of producers, consumers and decomposers including microorganisms
nodes.minimum_width: Construct a food web from a given organism list; identify producers, primary and secondary consumers, and decomposers including microorganisms
nodes.typical_width: Compare a food chain and food web for the same ecosystem — explain what information is lost in the simpler model
wider: Analyse food webs to show feeding relationships including the role of microorganisms in decomposition
Critique a published food web diagram — what is it missing, what assumptions does it make?
Investigate First Nations Australians' food webs in a specific region and compare with a scientific representation of the same ecosystem
success_criteria: Correctly places all organisms in a food web into producer, consumer or decomposer roles
Arrows show direction of energy flow, not just feeding relationships
Includes microorganisms in the decomposer role

nodes.label: Matter and energy flow
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can explain the direction and loss of energy through trophic levels and distinguish energy flow from matter cycling
nodes.minimum_width: Explain the direction of energy flow in a food web; state that energy is lost at each trophic level; distinguish energy flow from matter cycling
nodes.typical_width: Use a given energy pyramid to explain why top predators are rare; connect to food web structure
wider: Model how energy flows into and out of an ecosystem via food web pathways; represent quantitatively using the 10% rule
Compare energy flow in two different ecosystems (e.g. rainforest vs desert); explain structural differences
Analyse a real dataset of biomass at each trophic level; explain the pattern using energy flow principles
nodes.hinge_reason: Energy flow direction and loss is the conceptual engine behind all population prediction tasks. Students who conflate matter cycling with energy flow make systematic errors in Nodes 3 and 4. Primary misconception gateway for this standard.
success_criteria: States that energy is lost at each trophic level
Correctly distinguishes energy flow from matter cycling in an explanation
Traces the direction of energy through at least 3 trophic levels

nodes.label: Abiotic factors and population effects
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can predict and explain how a change to an abiotic factor cascades through populations in a food web
nodes.minimum_width: Define abiotic factor; give three examples; explain how one abiotic change would affect one population in a given food web
nodes.typical_width: Trace the cascade effect of one abiotic change (e.g. drought, temperature rise) through at least 3 populations with reasoning at each step
wider: Examine how seasonal changes or habitat destruction impact abiotic factors and cause population changes
Compare cascade effects of two different abiotic changes on the same ecosystem — which is more destabilising and why?
Investigate First Nations Australians' fire management practices and their effect on abiotic factors and flora/fauna distribution
success_criteria: Correctly defines abiotic factor and gives 3 examples
Predicts the effect of one abiotic change on one population with reasoning
Identifies at least one other population affected by the cascade

nodes.label: Biotic factors and population effects
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can predict and explain how a change to a biotic factor cascades through populations in a food web
nodes.minimum_width: Define biotic factor; give three examples; predict the effect of removing one species from a given food web with reasoning
nodes.typical_width: Analyse a trophic cascade case study (e.g. wolf reintroduction in Yellowstone); explain the chain of biotic effects with evidence
wider: Predict effects on local ecosystems when pollinators or predators are removed or introduced
Investigate First Nations Australians' responses to invasive species and their effect on food webs communities depend on
Evaluate an invasive species scenario — construct food webs before and after, predict population effects, and propose a management response
nodes.hinge_reason: Predicting biotic cascade effects requires holding the whole food web in mind simultaneously — the deepest application of the model. Assessment tasks typically test this directly. Students who haven't secured energy flow (Node 2) make compounding errors here.
success_criteria: Correctly defines biotic factor and gives 3 examples
Predicts the effect of removing one species with food web reasoning
Identifies at least two cascade effects beyond the immediate population

nodes.label: Models, limitations and predictions
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can evaluate the limitations of food web models and explain how these affect the reliability of predictions
nodes.minimum_width: Identify one limitation of food web models; explain how this affects the reliability of predictions made from them
nodes.typical_width: Compare a simple and complex model of the same ecosystem — explain what each can and cannot predict
wider: Evaluate a real scientific food web model used in a conservation decision — what did it predict, what did it miss?
Consider how introduction of a species impacts abiotic and biotic factors beyond immediate food web effects
Research how First Nations ecological knowledge has improved or challenged scientific food web models in a specific case
success_criteria: Identifies at least one limitation of food web models
Explains how that limitation affects the reliability of a specific prediction
